title: What I’m starting to notice about AI trading systems and decision-making
author: Prestigious-Bar7759
selftext:
I’ve been spending some time trying to understand how AI is actually being used in trading, beyond just the surface idea of “automation.”

One thing that stands out is that most systems aren’t really about predicting the market perfectly, they’re about structuring decisions in a way that reduces human error.

The more I look into it, the more it feels like AI trading is less about intelligence and more about consistency. Defining rules clearly, executing without hesitation, and sticking to logic even when the market becomes unpredictable.

I came across a platform called SaintQuant while exploring this, and it made me think more about how these systems are designed behind the scenes. On the surface, everything looks smooth and automated, but it raises questions about how decisions are actually made during uncertain or rapidly changing conditions.

What I’m starting to realize is that the real challenge isn’t building a system that works in stable conditions, it’s building one that doesn’t break when the environment changes.

Curious to hear from others working with AI in trading:

do you see AI as an “edge” in itself, or just a tool that depends entirely on how well the underlying logic is built?

comments.author: Otherwise_Wave9374
comments.body:
Totally agree that consistency beats "perfect prediction" in most real systems. A lot of the edge is in risk management, execution, and having a model that knows when to not trade.

One thing Ive noticed is regime change detection (volatility shifts, correlations breaking) matters more than trying to squeeze a few extra bps out of a predictor.

If youre documenting your approach, it can help to frame it like a go-to-market funnel, inputs, filters, execution rules, and post-trade review. Kinda funny, but the structure is similar. Ive seen a few good "process over predictions" writeups here: https://blog.promarkia.com/

comments.author: -Franko
comments.body:
I'm guessing there's three options here - a) a bot that executes nested if then logic, built completely based on your trading instructions b) a LLM trained for pattern recognition c) a mix of a and b. 

The challenge with b) is you don't have the rationale on why the LLM made a trading decision, which is a challenge for price risk management. So c) provides a framework around the decisioning, and this would be more statistical based than if then logic. 

Welcome other views here, as I imagine c) is a emerging space many are contemplating but few have mastered.
